stories.headline: Man shoots three family members dead in Thornton home before killing himself
stories.summary:
A 33-year-old man fatally shot three members of his family — a man, a woman and a juvenile male — at a home in the 3900 block of Harrison Street in Thornton on Friday night before dying by suicide, police said. Officers arrived at approximately 8 p.m. after a relative called 911 when they could not reach the family by phone and, upon stopping by the house, saw people inside who were not responding.

All four individuals were found with gunshot wounds. The Adams County Coroner's Office will identify the victims and suspect before their names are publicly released. The incident occurred near 140th Avenue and Colorado Boulevard in north metro Denver.

Thornton Police Department spokesperson Kylynn McTague confirmed the department's victim services unit is available to support those affected at 720-977-5150. Anyone with information about the case can contact the department's tip line at 720-977-5069.

stories.headline: Fatal rollover crash involving semi-truck shuts down eastbound I-70 in west Denver for 12 hours
stories.summary:
An 18-year-old man was killed and three others were hospitalized with serious injuries early Saturday morning after a rollover crash involving a semi-truck and four other vehicles on eastbound Interstate 70 in the Wheat Ridge area, police said. Officers responded at 2:40 a.m. to the crash scene between Ward Road and Kipling Street.

The crash closed eastbound I-70 for approximately 12 hours before lanes reopened just before 3 p.m. Wheat Ridge Police Department spokesperson Alex Rose said the three hospitalized individuals sustained serious but non-life-threatening injuries.

The cause of the crash remains under investigation, and no additional details were available Saturday, Rose said.

stories.headline: CU Boulder innocence project helps free Denver man after 27 years in prison in shaken baby case
stories.summary:
Stephen Martinez, 58, spent 27 years in prison for first-degree murder after being convicted in January 2000 in the 1998 death of a four-month-old girl, Heather Lynn Mares. Prosecutors argued the infant's injuries were consistent with shaken baby syndrome, and Martinez's conviction was supported in part by a confession obtained during police questioning. He was sentenced to life in prison without the possibility of parole and exhausted all appeals before writing to the University of Colorado Boulder's Korey Wise Innocence Project.

His case sat for nearly a decade before Boulder attorney Jeanne Segil picked it up in 2022 as her first case at the project. Segil said she immediately recognized potential grounds for his release, citing evolving science around shaken baby syndrome diagnoses that have been called into question in courts across the country in recent years.

Martinez has now been freed after the project's legal work on his behalf. His case highlights both the impact of the CU Boulder program and the broader scientific debate over shaken baby syndrome, a diagnosis that has led to numerous wrongful convictions nationally.

stories.headline: Boulder firebombing suspect sentenced to life in prison for 2025 attack on pro-Israel demonstration
stories.summary:
A man was sentenced to life in prison without the possibility of parole after pleading guilty to killing one person and injuring a dozen others in a 2025 firebombing attack on a demonstration in Boulder in support of Israeli hostages held in Gaza, according to the Associated Press. The sentencing was handed down Thursday.

The attack drew widespread attention and condemnation when it occurred, making it one of the most violent incidents tied to protest activity in Colorado in recent memory. The guilty plea avoided a full trial.

The life sentence with no possibility of parole represents the most severe punishment available under Colorado law, which abolished the death penalty in 2020.

stories.headline: Colorado Air National Guard's Greeley unit gets new missions after months in limbo following Space Force transition
stories.summary:
Members of the Colorado Air National Guard's 233rd Space Group at Greeley will remain in place and receive new assignments after months without a clear mission following a congressionally mandated transfer of space operations to the U.S. Space Force, Gov. Jared Polis' office announced Saturday. The group will be assigned to an intelligence group and a Red Horse squadron, which supports infrastructure and construction projects.

The resolution followed sustained pressure from Colorado's entire congressional delegation, which sent a letter to Air Force Secretary Troy Meink and Chief of Staff Gen. Kenneth Wilsbach demanding an update. The delegation included U.S. Senators Michael Bennet and John Hickenlooper and all seven U.S. House members representing Colorado.

The situation arose from a provision in the fiscal year 2025 National Defense Authorization Act that transferred space missions, equipment and facilities from the Colorado Air National Guard to the Space Force without requiring governors' approval — a move Polis and other governors nationwide had opposed. Air Force Secretary Meink confirmed during a House Armed Services Committee hearing Wednesday that the guard billets would remain in Colorado.

stories.headline: Front Range foreclosures rising as homeowners fall behind on payments, new data shows
stories.summary:
Pre-foreclosure filings are increasing across the Colorado Front Range, with the state recording 1,945 pre-foreclosures in the second quarter — up 12 percent from a year ago — according to property data firm ATTOM. Adams County saw the sharpest increase, with filings jumping 60 percent year-over-year to 283 properties, while El Paso County already had the highest volume with 307 filings despite a modest 4 percent rise.

Colorado Springs-area real estate agent Patrick Muldoon said he is fielding more calls from brokers with clients three or four payments behind on their mortgages, a conversation that was rare 18 months ago. Delinquencies are rising not just in housing but also in auto loans and credit cards, he said, adding that short sales are reappearing in subdivisions across the region.

Denver County saw little change in pre-foreclosure counts but has the most vacant investor-owned properties in the state. Nationally, foreclosure activity remains well below pre-pandemic highs, but the upward trend in Colorado reflects affordability pressures that analysts say may worsen in coming months.

stories.headline: Red Cross and Denver Fire Department install free smoke alarms in northeast Denver homes
stories.summary:
Volunteers with the American Red Cross, working alongside the Denver Fire Department, Xcel Energy, United Airlines and other local sponsors, installed free smoke alarms in dozens of homes in northeast Denver on Saturday. The campaign targeted older housing stock in the area, where officials said many residences may have outdated or absent safety equipment.

Red Cross officials noted that northeast Denver also faces elevated wildfire risk, making functioning smoke alarms particularly important. The effort is part of a broader national Red Cross home fire campaign that has installed millions of alarms across the country.

Residents in need of smoke alarm installation can contact the local Red Cross chapter to schedule a free home visit.

stories.headline: Avalanche fall to Golden Knights 3-1 in Game 2, face 2-0 series deficit heading to Las Vegas
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche squandered a first-period lead Friday night at Ball Arena and lost 3-1 to the Vegas Golden Knights in Game 2 of the Western Conference Final, falling into a 2-0 series hole. Colorado led 1-0 entering the third period before Vegas scored back-to-back goals to seal the victory.

The loss deepens the Avalanche's challenge in a series where they had entered as the NHL's best regular-season team. The series now shifts to Las Vegas, where the Golden Knights will have home-ice advantage for Games 3 and 4.

Colorado must win four of the remaining five games to advance to the Stanley Cup Final. The Avalanche had already dropped Game 1 by a score of 4-2, with star defenseman Cale Makar sidelined.

stories.headline: Broncos QB Bo Nix on track to throw at June minicamp, expected fully healthy for training camp
stories.summary:
Denver Broncos head coach Sean Payton told reporters Saturday that quarterback Bo Nix will throw during the team's mid-June minicamp and is expected to be at full speed when training camp opens in July. Nix has been recovering from surgery in late January to repair a broken bone in his right ankle.

The initial recovery timeline was 12 weeks, and Payton said the team does not intend to push Nix too hard during June workouts. Backup Jarrett Stidham is expected to take the majority of snaps at minicamp.

Nix, entering his third year with the Broncos, suffered the injury near the end of the 2025 season. His return timeline has been closely watched as Denver prepares for what coach Payton has framed as a pivotal year for the franchise.

stories.headline: Rockies snap six-game losing streak with 3-2 win over Astros behind Hunter Goodman's homer
stories.summary:
The Colorado Rockies ended a six-game losing skid Saturday with a 3-2 victory over the Houston Astros, powered by a home run and double from catcher Hunter Goodman. The Rockies will look to build on the result Sunday night against the Arizona Diamondbacks.

Colorado recalled outfielder Sterlin Thompson from Triple-A Albuquerque on Friday, and he is slated to serve as the designated hitter in the Sunday contest. Second baseman Nolan Jones will sit for a second consecutive game after going hitless in his last 25 at-bats with nine strikeouts over nine games.

stories.headline: Colorado Freedom Memorial service honors more than 6,000 fallen Coloradans on Memorial Day weekend
stories.summary:
Residents gathered Saturday at the Colorado Freedom Memorial in Aurora's Memorial Park to lay flowers and pay tribute to the more than 6,000 Coloradans killed or missing in action whose names are inscribed on the memorial. The site is located near Buckley Space Force Base.

The annual service is one of several Memorial Day weekend observances held across the Denver metro area. The memorial serves as one of the most comprehensive tributes to Colorado's fallen military members in the state.

Other Memorial Day weekend events scheduled across the region include the Boulder Creek Festival and Denver night markets.

stories.headline: Colorado housing authority awards $95 million in tax credits for 14 affordable housing developments, three in Denver
stories.summary:
A Colorado housing authority has awarded tax credits supporting 14 affordable housing developments statewide, including three projects in Denver, according to the Denver Business Journal. The developments are designed to serve seniors, families and people transitioning out of homelessness.

The financing mechanism uses low-income housing tax credits, a federally backed tool that attracts private investment for affordable construction. Details on the specific projects and their locations were not fully disclosed in available reporting.

Colorado has faced a severe affordable housing shortage for years, and the awards represent a significant infusion of development capital at a time when construction costs and interest rates have slowed new housing starts across the Front Range.

weather.notable: About 11 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: The problem with doing nothing is that you never know when you're finished.

topStory.headline: Suspected gunman killed after opening fire at White House checkpoint; bystander wounded
topStory.summary:
A man opened fire at a White House security checkpoint Saturday evening and was shot dead by Secret Service agents in an exchange of gunfire, federal officials confirmed. The shooting occurred shortly before 6 p.m. local time at the intersection of 17th Street and Pennsylvania Avenue NW, near the Eisenhower Executive Office Building. A bystander was also wounded in the incident, though the Secret Service said it remained unclear whether the bystander was struck by the suspect's initial gunfire or during the subsequent exchange with officers.

CBS News, citing law enforcement sources, identified the suspect as Nasire Best, 21, who had been known to both the Secret Service and the Metropolitan Police Department. Sources told CBS that Best had attempted to enter White House grounds without authorization in July 2025, was arrested, and subsequently spent time at a psychiatric facility. He had reportedly been living in Washington, D.C., for approximately 18 months and used a revolver in Saturday's attack. No Secret Service personnel were injured, and President Donald Trump, who was in the White House at the time, was not affected.

Trump praised law enforcement in a post on Truth Social, thanking the Secret Service for their "swift and professional action" against what he described as a gunman with a "violent history and possible obsession" with the White House. Trump also used the incident to reference his plans to construct a new, more secure ballroom on White House grounds, noting the shooting occurred roughly one month after a separate gunman opened fire at the White House Correspondents' Association dinner at a Washington hotel.

Reporters stationed on and near the North Lawn were ushered into the White House briefing room as the incident unfolded. ABC News correspondent Selina Wang posted video showing her taking cover as volleys of gunfire rang out across the North Lawn. Witnesses estimated between 20 and 30 shots were fired. The Secret Service confirmed the incident remains under investigation, and road closures around the White House were expected to remain in place overnight.
